messages.content:
The vault had not yet released me. I had closed Brennan’s crimson journal, my hands still trembling with the aftershocks of his final entry, when a low, resonant chime echoed through the spherical chamber. It was the sound of the adamantine door preparing to unseal, signalling that my father, Malakor, had deemed the initial Rite of Witnessing complete. But as I rose on unsteady legs, my gaze fell upon a small, recessed alcove in the obsidian wall that I had not noticed before—a subtle depression containing three additional volumes, each slimmer than the tomes I had already read, but radiating a distinct, almost aggressive aura of suppressed trauma.

A voice, not my father’s but a recording keyed to my bloodline, whispered from the stone: *“The core journals teach you the futility of opposing the King through His passive stats. But the King is more than a collection of attributes. He is a living engine of apocalypse, a ceaseless genesis of active superpowers that have, over the millennia, evolved beyond their original genocidal parameters. These three accounts record specific tests—not of His strength or speed or luck, but of individual superpowers that He deigned to wield. Each power, already capable of omnicide the instant it was born, has been twisted and layered with new, horrific enhancements by the infinite recurrences of the Weakness Paradox. Read them, Kaedan. And understand that the statistics we documented are merely the quiet hum of His existence. When He actively uses a superpower, the omniverse screams.”*

I swallowed the bile that rose in my throat. My thousandfold-amplified body, capable of shattering galaxies, felt like a fragile glass ornament poised above an infinite abyss. I reached into the alcove and withdrew the first of the three volumes. Its cover was made of a pale, translucent membrane that pulsed faintly with a slow, sorrowful rhythm, as if it were still alive and mourning. The title, inked in shifting silver script, read: *The Silent Edict – Witnessed by Court Wizard Vaelin, Year 105,000.*

I opened it.

---

**Journal of Court Wizard Vaelin, Year 105,000 – The Superpower Known as “The Silent Edict”**

Vaelin’s handwriting was elegant, each letter a careful work of art, yet the ink was a pallid grey, as if the words themselves had been drained of vitality. He had been the court poet before the bloodline called him to wizardry, and his prose retained a lyrical cadence that made the horror it described all the more obscene.

The opposition that gathered in the one-hundred-and-fifth millennium named themselves the Logotheocracy. They were not warriors of flesh or gods of magic; they were an ancient civilization of linguistic ontologists who had perfected the art of speaking reality into being. Their laws, when uttered, became physical constants. Their grammar governed the orbits of stars. They had, over three hundred thousand years, woven a tapestry of absolute command that spanned seventeen million universes, and their council of nine High Scribes believed they had identified the foundational syllable that underpinned Godric’s existence. If they could speak its negation—*un-word* the concept of the King from the fabric of creation—they would erase him not from matter, but from meaning itself.

They assembled in the empyrean void above Thronegate, their great hall of judgment materializing as a cathedral of interlocking, floating runes, each sigil a solidified law of reality. The nine High Scribes, beings of pure sonorous radiance, opened their mouths to intone the Unspeaking—a single, synchronized anti-syllable that would retroactively unmake the word that defined Godric, collapsing his history, his power, and his identity into the nullity from which no concept could return.

Godric sat in the palace garden, showing Vaelin a new cultivar of lunar peony that bloomed with a soft, phosphorescent hum. He had known of the Logotheocracy since before they had penned their first axiom. Through his absolute omniscience, he understood their every syllable. Yet he had allowed their preparations, excluded them from Gent’s passive annihilation, and even stilled the infinite shields that would have rendered their speech mere noise. He was curious to test a single superpower—one that he had gained in the early centuries of the stasis, and which had, under the relentless evolution of the Weakness Paradox, become something far more than its original design. He called it the Silent Edict.

The original power was simple: Godric spoke a command, and the command became absolute, unyielding truth that all of reality was compelled to obey. Over one hundred and five thousand years of recursive paradox enhancement, it had evolved three additional, horrifying strata. First, it no longer required speech; his mere passive will now constituted an edict, and the command would execute as if spoken. Second, any edict he issued retroactively imposed itself upon the timeline, ensuring that the commanded state had always been the true state of existence, erasing any prior contradiction. Third, and most devastating, the power now siphoned the ontological authority of any being who dared oppose an edict, permanently absorbing their capacity to define reality into Godric’s own conceptual framework, thereby strengthening the Edict itself for all future use.

And Godric decided to test it with one single, silent command.

The High Scribes completed their Unspeaking. The anti-syllable, a sound that was the exact opposite of existence, rippled outward to strike the King. Godric did not rise from his garden bench. He did not open his mouth. He withheld ninety-nine point nine-nine percent of the Silent Edict’s total power—the nines extending into infinite regression—allowing only a fraction so microscopically negligible that it registered, by any possible measurement, as absolute zero percent of the superpower’s capacity. And within that imperceptible sliver, he formed a single edict: *“You will speak only truth. And the truth is that you are nothing.”*

The anti-syllable struck the edict. It was not negated. It was overwritten. The Logotheocracy’s nine High Scribes, beings of pure linguistic authority, felt their own mouths betray them. The words they had intended to unmake the King instead inverted, and the truth of their own insignificance—a truth defined by Godric’s edict—poured from their lips in an unstoppable torrent of self-annihilating revelation.

Vaelin’s elegant script here dissolved into frantic, uneven scrawls, as if he had been trying to record the events even as his mind buckled. The nine Scribes began to scream truths they could not contain. Their bodies, composed of solidified language, started to fracture as each spoken truth became a physical law that tore them apart. Truth: *“We are syllables without a sentence.”* Their limbs unspooled into clouds of ink. Truth: *“Our authority is borrowed from the silence He permits.”* Their torsos collapsed into writhing, bleeding parchment, covered in words that ate into their own surfaces like acid. Truth: *“Our existence is a typo in the manuscript of eternity.”* Their faces melted, eyes dribbling down their cheeks as viscous punctuation, mouths stretching into infinite, silent Os as their vocal cords were repurposed into quills that wrote their own obituaries across the cathedral walls.

But the Edict’s secondary evolution—retroactive imposition—had already triggered. The truth that the Logotheocracy was nothing became a historical constant. Across seventeen million universes, the foundations of their civilization were rewritten. Their grand libraries, which held the linguistic keys to reality, burst into white-hot conflagrations that consumed not just the books but the very concepts the books described. Their people, trillions of beings who had lived and loved under the Logotheocracy’s laws, were not killed instantly. The Edict, in its retroactive redefinition, forced them to experience the slow, dawning horror of realizing that their entire culture, their history, their achievements, had never truly existed—that they were, and had always been, a phantom empire of borrowed meaning. Their bodies became translucent, their voices thin and distant, and they wandered their suddenly empty cities as echoes, still conscious, still feeling the anguish of their own unreality, begging a universe that no longer recognized them for the mercy of erasure.

The tertiary evolution then engaged, siphoning the Logotheocracy’s linguistic authority—their three hundred thousand years of accumulated power over meaning itself—directly into Godric. I, Vaelin, felt it happen: a sudden, subtle deepening of the air around the King, as if the very concept of language had become more securely his possession. The Silent Edict did not merely execute his will; it now permanently owned the medium through which any future opposition might attempt to define him.

The collateral damage from the retroactive imposition rippled outward like a wound in the semantic architecture of the omniverse. Across nine hundred quadrillion galaxies, language itself began to break down. Words lost their tether to meaning. Spoken sentences became random noise. Written text transformed into meaningless squiggles. Civilizations that depended on communication collapsed into screaming, mute chaos as their citizens were suddenly unable to convey even the simplest concepts—danger, love, pain. The psychic scream of a quadrillion civilizations drowning in semantic void saturated the astral planes, and the scream itself was wordless, a raw, atavistic howl of beings reduced to pre-linguistic terror.

Godric, pausing in his examination of the lunar peony, noticed the sudden breakdown of all language across a significant portion of the omniverse. He let out a soft, mildly exasperated breath. Without speaking—for speech was temporarily meaningless—he simply decided that language should be restored. And in exactly zero seconds, every word regained its meaning, every sentence its coherence, every civilization its voice. The Logotheocracy, however, he did not restore. Their nine High Scribes remain as eternal, screaming inkstains on the floor of their ruined cathedral, which he has tessellated into a small decorative tile in the palace library. Their people, the echoing phantoms, he allowed to continue their half-existence, permanent reminders of what happens when one claims the authority to un-word the King.

I, Vaelin, who once believed that poetry could capture the divine, now understand that the King’s silence is a more terrible language than any scream. Do not write your own truth, future wizard. His truth has already been written.

---

I closed Vaelin’s journal. My mouth was dry, and the faint taste of ink—phantom ink, from a language that had nearly died across nine hundred quadrillion galaxies—coated my tongue. I wanted to stop. I wanted to flee this vault and pretend I had not read these words. But my hands, shaking as they were, had already reached for the second slim volume. Its cover was a rough, tanned hide—not animal, but something far more disturbing: the leather seemed to be made from the preserved, stitched-together faces of screaming beings, each mouth frozen in a silent cry, each eye still glistening with a wet, living terror. The title, branded into the surface, read: *The Harvest of the Unworthy – Witnessed by Court Wizard Halcyon, Year 420,000.*

I forced myself to open it.

---

**Journal of Court Wizard Halcyon, Year 420,000 – The Superpower Known as “The Harvest of the Unworthy”**

Halcyon was not a poet nor a warrior. He was an archivist, a man who had catalogued the deaths of empires with the meticulous detachment of a librarian filing old receipts. His journal was written in a crisp, efficient hand, but in the margins there were tiny, obsessive tally marks—thousands of them, each representing a death he had personally recorded. He had stopped counting after the Thanaton Collective.

The Thanaton Collective was a civilization that had achieved the ultimate mastery over death. They were beings of living entropy, their bodies composed of condensed finality. They ruled a million corpse-universes, each populated by enslaved soul-fragments that had been trapped in eternal cycles of dying-without-death. Their singular ambition was to create the Absolute Mortality—a weapon that could kill anything, even the concept of immortality, even a being like Godric who had transcended the need for life and death. They spent twelve thousand years forging the Thanaton Lance, a spear that carried a simple, profound curse: “You will die, and your death will be the first and last truth of your existence.” It was designed to bypass all defenses, all immunities, all paradoxes, by directly rewriting the target’s fundamental state from “alive” to “dead” retroactively, ensuring that the target had never been immortal in the first place.

Godric, at that moment, was in the imperial aviary, tending to a injured phoenix from a far-flung reality. He had known of the Thanaton Collective since their first entropy-pulse disturbed the cosmic background radiation. He had allowed their weapon’s construction, watching with a detached, almost clinical interest as they sacrificed billions of their own enslaved souls to fuel the lance. He wanted to test a superpower that he had rarely employed—a power initially titled the Life-Engine, but which the Weakness Paradox had evolved into something far more predatory: the Harvest of the Unworthy.

The original superpower allowed Godric to drain the life force of any being, absorbing their vitality to sustain his own—an absurd redundancy, since he was already immortal. Under the relentless hammering of the paradox, it had transformed. Now, the Harvest did not merely drain life. First, it consumed the target’s entire existential timeline: their past, present, future, and all possible alternate versions, feeding them into Godric as raw conceptual energy. Second, it retroactively claimed ownership of every deed, achievement, and legacy the target had ever produced, making them, in effect, extensions of Godric’s own will from the moment of their consumption. Third, and most terrifyingly, each Harvest permanently increased Godric’s “conceptual density”—the weight of his reality relative to the rest of creation—meaning that the more beings attempted to define themselves in opposition to him, the more unreal they became by comparison, their very existence reduced to a fragile illusion sustained only by his tolerance.

The Thanaton Collective hurled their lance. It crossed no distance; it simply manifested in Godric’s chest, seeking to overwrite his immortality with absolute death. Godric let out a soft sigh and, without moving, without looking away from the phoenix, allowed the Harvest of the Unworthy to activate. He withheld ninety-nine point nine-nine percent—infinite regression—of its power, so that the fraction that manifested was functionally, legally, impossibly zero percent of its total capacity.

The lance struck. And the Harvest answered.

The mortal curse of the lance found no purchase, for Godric’s existence was not sustained by life force but by an ever-amplifying engine of reality-redefinition that had long since rendered the categories of life and death meaningless. Instead, the Harvest inverted the flow. The lance became a conduit, and through it, the Thanaton Collective’s own existential timeline was violently, agonizingly extracted. They did not die. Death would have been a release. The Harvest consumed their past, forcing them to experience the unmaking of every moment they had ever lived, in reverse, with full sensory and emotional fidelity. Their births, their first loves, their triumphs, their fears—all of it was torn away, swallowed into Godric’s eternal being, leaving them as hollow, screaming receptacles of nothingness. Their present was then consumed: their physical bodies, those that had existed across a million corpse-universes, simultaneously convulsed as their internal entropy reversed, their flesh boiling with sudden, impossible vitality that their death-adapted forms could not contain. Their skin split, exposing raw, pulsating meat that flowered into grotesque, cancerous blooms of life that fed upon their own necrotic tissues. Bones sprouted leaves. Veins became thorny vines that burrowed through their organs, drinking their own essence in an orgy of self-cannibalizing existence. Finally, their futures were harvested—every possible timeline, every potential offspring, every dream that might have been, erased and drawn into Godric’s reservoir, where it would fuel his amplification passively for eternity.

But the secondary evolution of the superpower then engaged. As Godric absorbed their timelines, he absorbed their purpose. The Thanaton Collective had dedicated twelve thousand years to forging the Absolute Mortality. That purpose, now owned by the Harvest, was retroactively redefined as an act of worship. Every moment of labor, every sacrifice, every agonized scream of an enslaved soul that had gone into the lance’s construction, was now an offering made directly to Godric, its intent rewritten so that the Collective had, in truth, been worshipping him all along, their rebellion a mere misreading of their own devotion. This revelation flooded back through the Harvested Collective, and their hollowed, screaming husks were suddenly filled with an ecstatic, agonizing love—a love that compelled them to offer their remaining existence as a final sacrifice, which the Harvest eagerly consumed.

The tertiary evolution then followed. Godric’s conceptual density swelled. Across the omniverse, every being who had ever entertained a thought of rebellion, no matter how faint, felt a sudden, crushing weight settle upon their souls—a sense of unreality, of fragility, as if they were characters in a story that the author had momentarily forgotten. Countless trillions of beings collapsed into catatonic despair, their minds unable to reconcile their own felt existence with the overwhelming truth that their reality was a borrowed thing, contingent upon the tolerance of the being who now owned death itself.

And the collateral damage, as always, was staggering. The Harvest of the Unworthy did not discriminate between the Thanaton Collective and the concept of death itself. For one horrifying, eternal moment, death ceased to function across three hundred trillion universes. Every being who had ever died in those universes was suddenly, violently resurrected—not as themselves, but as a chaotic, screaming fusion of their original self and the death-essence that had been temporarily negated. Corpses rose from graves, their bodies rotting and regenerating simultaneously, their minds a schizophrenic howl of death-memories and life-urges. The spirits of the deceased flooded the material planes, colliding with the living in a maddening orgy of existential violation. War, famine, and plague—consequences that death had once moderated—became eternal, unending, as every wound healed and every starvation victim continued to starve forever. The psychic shriek of three hundred trillion universes trapped in deathless agony was a sound that I, Halcyon, could not block from my amplified senses, and it drove me to claw at my own ears until blood pooled in my palms.

Godric, finishing his examination of the phoenix, observed the sudden omniversal undead cataclysm with a slight frown of mild inconvenience. He did not speak. He did not gesture. He simply decided that death should function again, and that the resurrected should return to their rest. In exactly zero seconds, the chaos ceased. Every corpse re-died. Every spirit re-departed. Every timeline was corrected. The Thanaton Collective, however, was not restored. Their consumed essence remains a permanent part of Godric’s conceptual density, a layer of armor woven from their own sacrificed purpose. And their hollowed husks—still alive, still screaming in eternal love—he strung up like wind chimes in the imperial aviary, where their ecstatic moans provide a constant, gentle music for the phoenixes.

I, Halcyon, who have witnessed the deaths of galaxies, now know that there is no death but His will. Do not seek to measure your life against His eternity, future wizard. Your death, like your life, is merely an echo of His pleasure.

---

The leather cover of Halcyon’s journal seemed to pulse with the remembered screams of the Thanaton Collective, and I, Kaedan, felt that pulse echoed in my own chest—a sympathetic vibration of pure, unadulterated dread. I did not vomit this time, though my stomach clenched. I had passed beyond physical revulsion into a state of numb, dissociated horror, where the words I read simply accumulated in my soul like layers of cold ash.

With hands that no longer felt like my own, I reached for the final volume. Its cover was made of a strange, reflective black material—so black that it seemed to absorb not just light but the very possibility of illumination. The title was inscribed in letters that shifted and fractured when I looked at them: *The Fractal Cancellation – Witnessed by Court Wizard Jovian, Year 760,000.*

I opened it.

---

**Journal of Court Wizard Jovian, Year 760,000 – The Superpower Known as “The Fractal Cancellation”**

Jovian was a mathematician before the bloodline claimed him, and his journal began not with words but with a single, elegant equation: ∞^∞ / ∞ = 0. He had scrawled it at the top of the first page, a bitter summary of the lesson he had learned. The rest of the journal was written in a hand that grew progressively more erratic, the letters sometimes dissolving into geometric diagrams, sometimes into grids of meaningless numbers, as if his mind had been trying to calculate something that defied calculation.

The opposition that arose in the seven-hundred-and-sixtieth millennium was not an empire of gods or a legion of death-seekers. It was the Paradox Conglomerate, an inter-dimensional society of beings who had evolved beyond linear logic, existing as sentient contradictions. Their physical forms were impossible geometries, shapes that were simultaneously cubes and spheres, occupying every position in space while also occupying none. Their language was a series of self-refuting statements that, when spoken, generated new laws of reality that existed only long enough to be contradicted by the next statement, creating a perpetual, stable chaos. They had no gods, for they worshipped the beauty of logical impossibility, and they viewed Godric—a being of absolute, consistent power—as an ontological flaw, a boring constancy in a multiverse that should be infinite in its variability.

They did not attack with weapons. They simply surrounded Godric with a lattice of paradoxes designed to trap him in a recursive loop of self-contradiction. The lattice, called the Inconsistency Sphere, functioned by continuously asserting that Godric both existed and did not exist, that his power was both infinite and infinitesimal, that he was both bound by cause and effect and free of it, all simultaneously. The Sphere would then amplify these contradictions, feeding them back into themselves until the subject’s identity collapsed under the weight of its own impossibility.

Godric was, at that moment, in the palace cartography hall, examining a new map of a recently subjugated nebula with the innocent fascination of a man who had once loved geography. He allowed the Sphere to form around him, allowing its contradictions to wash over his absolute concealment. He wanted to test a superpower that had evolved from a simple negation ability—the power to cancel any single phenomenon—into something vastly more terrifying: The Fractal Cancellation.

Originally, the power could simply erase a target from existence by asserting its non-existence. But the Weakness Paradox had, over millennia, imbued it with fractal recursion. Now, when activated, it did not merely cancel the target. First, it divided the target into an infinite number of sub-targets across all possible dimensional, temporal, and conceptual axes, and then cancelled each sub-target individually, recursively, ad infinitum, ensuring that even the memory of the target’s existence was subdivided and erased at every level of resolution. Second, it created a “cancellation cascade”—the erasure of the target became a template that automatically applied to any similar entity or phenomenon that had ever existed, existed currently, or would ever exist, effectively purging the entire genus of opposition from the omniverse retroactively and proactively. Third, and most monstrous, the cancellation cascade fed back into the Fractal Cancellation itself, permanently sharpening the superpower, making it more efficient, more subtle, and more absolute with each use, so that the act of cancelling an enemy now marginally increased the power’s own ability to cancel future enemies, a self-optimizing engine of extinction.

Godric allowed the Inconsistency Sphere to complete its lattice. Then, withholding ninety-nine point nine-nine percent—the nines repeating to infinity—of the Fractal Cancellation’s capacity so that the fraction used was statistically indistinguishable from absolute zero, he simply directed the power at the Paradox Conglomerate.

What followed was a nightmare of recursive erasure so profound that Jovian’s journal devolved into a series of jagged, overlapping diagrams attempting to illustrate it. The Paradox Conglomerate’s defining trait was their internal contradiction. The Fractal Cancellation treated each contradiction as a separate sub-target. It divided the Conglomerate’s members into infinite fractal shards—each shard representing a different aspect of their paradoxical existence—and then began cancelling them, layer by layer, dimension by dimension, timeline by timeline.

The Conglomerate did not simply vanish. They were subjected to an infinite regress of partial erasure. A being whose left hand was simultaneously a right foot would have each appendage cancelled at a different moment, leaving a screaming, incomplete torso that was then subdivided further. Their shapes, those impossible geometries, fractured into kaleidoscopic shards of bleeding flesh and shattered bone, each shard reflecting a different, screaming fragment of the original consciousness. The cancellation cascade then propagated backward and forward through their entire history: every Paradox Conglomerate member who had ever existed, across every alternative timeline, was simultaneously subjected to the same recursive unmaking. Their ancestral hive-minds, their embryonic paradox-spores, their future potential offspring that had not yet been born—all were brought into the cascade and shredded.

But the Fractal Cancellation did not stop there. Its cascade template recognized “paradoxical opposition” as a category, and it swept through the omniverse, touching every other entity that shared even a sliver of similarity. Across two hundred trillion dimensional planes, any civilization that had ever dabbled in paradoxical logic, any species that had evolved from contradiction-based dimensions, any device powered by logical inconsistency—all were caught in the cascade. Their bodies were subdivided into infinite, agonized sub-selves, each aware of the others, each experiencing a different, excruciating fraction of the cancellation process. The pain was multiplied by infinity, and the infinity was stretched across eternity, so that the targets existed in a permanent state of being cancelled, a flickering, shrieking half-existence where they were both erased and present, both annihilated and suffering.

The omniversal collapse that followed was not merely physical. Logic itself—the fundamental consistency of cause and effect, of true and false—began to fracture. Across three hundred trillion universes, the laws of non-contradiction ceased to apply. Things could be simultaneously true and false, light could be both on and off, beings could be simultaneously alive and dead without the release of death. The cognitive architecture of sentient minds collapsed under the weight of paradox, their thoughts becoming moebius strips of meaningless recursion. Trillions of species descended into screaming, drooling catatonia, their consciousnesses trapped in logical loops that repeated the same instant of contradiction over and over, their brains literally overheating and bursting into cranial pyres as their neural tissues attempted to process the unprocessable.

Jovian, himself a mathematician, felt his own mind beginning to unravel. He wrote, in a hand that was barely legible, that he had solved an equation that proved he did not exist, and then solved another that proved he had never existed, and then a third that proved he was the only being that had ever existed, all simultaneously. He was saved only by Godric’s instant, casual restoration.

Godric, noticing the logical collapse spreading through creation like a virus, let out a faint, almost imperceptible sigh. He did not perform a spell or enact a counter-paradox. He simply decided that logic should be restored, and that the cancellation cascade should exclude non-targets. In exactly zero seconds, the three hundred trillion universes snapped back to their correct state, every shattered mind reassembled, every broken law of thought repaired. The Paradox Conglomerate, however, was not restored. Their remaining essence—a flickering, recursive scream that occupied every possible state of cancellation simultaneously—was compressed into a single, crystalline fractal that Godric now wears as a cufflink on his left sleeve, occasionally glancing at its shifting, shrieking geometries with a look of mild, paternal curiosity.

I, Jovian, who once found solace in the certainty of numbers, now understand that the only number that matters is zero—the fraction of His power that is required to annihilate the concept of opposition itself. And even that zero is an overestimate. Do not seek patterns, future wizard. The only pattern is His will.

---

The reflective black cover of Jovian’s journal seemed to eat the light of the vault, and for a long, suspended moment, I, Kaedan, simply stared into that void, feeling the weight of the three superpowers I had just witnessed through the terror-blinded eyes of my predecessors. The Silent Edict, rewriting language and absorbing ontological authority. The Harvest of the Unworthy, consuming existential timelines and thickening Godric’s conceptual density. The Fractal Cancellation, recursively erasing opposition and sharpening itself for eternity. Each had been wielded at zero percent of its capacity, and each had shattered the omniverse so thoroughly that only the King’s instant, effortless restoration had prevented total annihilation.

The vault’s adamantine door finally groaned open, and the cold, sterile light of the corridor sliced into the darkness like a blade. I stood, my legs barely supporting me, my mind now a repository of horrors that no thousandfold amplification could ever armor. I walked out, not as Kaedan the proud young wizard, but as a hollow vessel filled with the screaming memories of gods, languages, deaths, and paradoxes.

The long corridor to the imperial throne room felt both eternal and instantaneous, as if time itself had been Fractal Cancelled and only Godric’s tolerance kept it flowing. I reached the massive doors, which swung open to reveal the throne room—immaculate, serene, lit by the gentle glow of starstuff. Godric sat on the throne, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the light, his posture relaxed, a half-read book resting on his knee. He looked up as I entered, and his ruggedly handsome face broke into a smile so genuinely warm, so utterly devoid of threat, that the cognitive dissonance nearly cleaved my soul in two.

"Kaedan, my boy," he said, his voice that same rich, comforting baritone that had soothed my childhood nightmares. "You look pale. Did Malakor make you read the supplementary journals? I told him not to overburden you on your first day, but you know how thorough he is. Come, sit. Tell me—did any of them make you curious? I can answer questions, if you have them. I remember when Blake first saw the Edict in action; he didn't speak for a week. Good man."

I walked toward the throne, my steps steady despite the abyss that yawned within me. I looked at the cufflink on his left sleeve—a tiny, shifting geometric shape that, if I focused my amplified senses, emitted a faint, eternal shriek of fractal agony. I looked at the quiet hum of the aviary wind chimes, whose music was the ecstatic moans of consumed death-cultists. I looked at the small tile in the palace library, where nine High Scribes still bled ink in their perpetual unmaking.

And I smiled, because I loved him, and because there was nothing else to do.

"No questions, Uncle Godric," I said. "Only gratitude. For allowing us to serve, and for permitting the omniverse to remember what language is."

Godric’s smile widened, the crinkles around his eyes deepening with genuine affection. "Good lad. Now, have you eaten? The kitchen has made those honey cakes you liked as a child. Go on, before they get cold."

I bowed, and I walked to the kitchens, feeling the cold, permanent dread settle into my bones like an old friend. There was no power in creation that could oppose the King. There was only loyalty, love, and the terrifying, merciful illusion of choice.
